title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a community radio station. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: On Air: The Voice of the Community

texts.C:
### Julian, The Late-Night Jazz Show
'I wanted to share my record collection with the world,' Julian says. [51]The hardest part was the technical learning curve of managing the soundboard while simultaneously talking and monitoring levels.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my public speaking skills in my day job as a lawyer became much more fluid and relaxed.[/48] [53]I learned that radio is an intimate medium; you are talking to one person at a time, not a crowd.[/53] [50]I realized that my background in research helped me find fascinating anecdotes about the musicians I played.[/50] [47]Initially, the intense silence of the soundproof studio was quite a shock to my senses.[/47] It was a rhythmic escape.
texts.A:
### Sarah, The Local News Reporter
Sarah covered community events and local politics. [49]The experience taught me that every resident has a story worth telling if you only take the time to listen.[/49] [52]I hadn't anticipated the deep sense of connection I’d feel to my town after interviewing so many different people.[/52] [47]I was quite startled by how much the 'red light' anxiety affected my voice in the first few weeks.[/47] [54]What I valued most was the feedback from elderly listeners, who called in to share their memories of the town.[/54] [56]If I hadn't seen a 'volunteers wanted' sign in the local library, I’d never have stepped inside.[/56] It was a social awakening.
texts.B:
### Victor, The Technical Engineer
Victor ensured the station stayed on the air. 'It’s about the signal,' he notes. [51]We struggled with the aging equipment; maintaining a steady broadcast with gear from the 90s was a constant battle.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general problem-solving speed and ability to stay calm reached an all-time high, which was a great side effect.[/48] [53]I realized that radio is a vital lifeline for people who are isolated or lonely.[/53] [50]I found that the discipline I used in my previous career as an IT consultant was perfect for troubleshooting the digital stream.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the cramped, windowless control room, which I found quite oppressive at first.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried I wouldn't have anything to say on air.
texts.D:
### Elena, The Youth Program Mentor
'I helped teenagers produce their own podcasts,' Elena says. [49]The project made me realize that giving young people a platform is the best way to foster community engagement.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have found the station if my former colleague hadn't recommended me for the mentorship role.[/56] [54]I was moved by the creativity of the kids, who used sound effects in ways I’d never have imagined.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the physical exhaustion of the high-energy recording sessions to be so draining initially.[/47] [55]I found the initial task of teaching the ethics of journalism to sixteen-year-olds incredibly challenging.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that I wouldn't be 'cool' enough to relate to them.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or psychological impact of the radio environment or the 'on-air' experience?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) mentions the shock of the studio silence, Sarah (B) the 'red light' anxiety, and Elena (D) the exhaustion of sessions.

questions.question: notes a positive change in their professional skills or mental composure as a result of the work?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) saw his lawyer skills improve, and Victor (C) saw his problem-solving speed reach an all-time high.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perspective on the value of every individual's story or the importance of youth platforms?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) reflects on every resident's story, and Elena (D) on youth platforms fostering engagement.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or previous career was advantageous to their role?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) used his research skills, and Victor (C) used his IT consultant discipline.

questions.question: describes a challenge related to technical multitasking or the state of the equipment?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) mentions managing the soundboard while talking, and Victor (C) the aging equipment.

questions.question: admits to having had prior doubts or having formed an unexpected emotional bond with their surroundings?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) felt a deep connection to her town, and Elena (D) worried about being 'cool' enough.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about the intimacy of the medium or its role as a lifeline?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) learned about radio's intimacy, and Victor (C) saw it as a lifeline for the isolated.

questions.question: highlights the value of the feedback from listeners or the creativity of a specific group?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) valued the feedback from elderly listeners, and Elena (D) the creativity of the kids.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the physical space or the teaching of complex topics?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) adjusted to the windowless room, and Elena (D) found teaching ethics challenging.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external factor or person that led to their involvement?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) saw a sign in the library, and Elena (D) was recommended by a colleague.
